mūla.devanāgarī: गाम् आविश्य च भूतानि धारयाम्य् अहम् ओजसा | पुष्णामि चौषधीः सर्वाः सोमो भूत्वा रसात्मकः
mūla.iast: gām āviśya ca bhūtāni dhārayāmy aham ojasā | puṣṇāmi cauṣadhīḥ sarvāḥ somo bhūtvā rasātmakaḥ
mūla.chapter_position: Chapter 15 (Puruṣottama-Yoga (The Yoga of the Supreme Person)), verse 13
mūla.speaker: Krishna

senses_attested_in_panel.sense: प्रविश्य धारयामि भूतानि जगत् अहम् ओजसा बलेन
सर्वाणि भूतानि ओजसा मम अप्रतिहतसामर्थ्येन धारयामि
धारयामि? अहमेव रसमयः सोमो भूत्वा व्रीह्याद्यौषधीः सर्वाः संवर्धयामि

word_by_word.grammar: 1st person singular present indicative active verb

senses_attested_in_panel.sense: पुष्टिमतीः रसस्वादुमतीश्च करोमि सोमो भूत्वा रसात्मकः सोमः सन् रसात्मकः रसस्वभावः
ओषधीः सर्वा व्रीहियवाद्याः पृथिव्यां जाता अहमेव पुष्णामि पुष्टिमतीः स्वादुमतीश्च करोमि

corpus_provenance.mūla: Belvalkar critical edition (BORI 1947), via Ambuda multi-witness

so_what_questions: If the same Paramātman who upholds the earth also nourishes the grain, what remains of 'natural causation' as an independent category — and does collapsing that category liberate or merely confuse?

Madhva insists the upholding power belongs to Hari alone and the jīva is purely paratantra (dependent). Does complete dependence on a sovereign Lord make ethical agency possible or does it dissolve it?
Vallabha's Soma is 'amṛtamaya' — made of nectar-delight. What would it mean to eat a meal with the awareness that the flavour itself is a form of Kṛṣṇa's rasa rather than a chemical property?

Śaṅkara cites the Vedic mantra 'yeṇa dyaur ugrā pṛthivī ca dṛḍhā' to anchor this verse in śruti. What kind of authority does a Vedic mantra carry when functioning as an empirical claim about physical cohesion?

Madhusūdana uses the image of earth crumbling like a 'fistful of sand' without divine ojas. What is the experiential difference between living as if the world is self-sustaining versus living as if it is held moment-to-moment by the Lord?

Rāmānuja reads the earth and its herbs as real modes of Bhagavān's body. Does this make environmental destruction a form of violence against the Lord's body — and does Viśiṣṭādvaita theology actually commit to that implication?

All six schools agree that the 'I' who nourishes is Bhagavān, not a secondary agent. How does that unanimity on attribution sit alongside the sharp disagreement on whether that Bhagavān is personal, qualified-non-dual, or strictly non-dual?

everyday_applications.advaita: When you feel crushed under the weight of responsibilities you cannot discharge, recall Śaṅkara's ojas — the desireless (kāma-rāga-vivarjita) power that holds the earth without effort or anxiety. The load you carry is not truly yours; investigate who the carrier is.
everyday_applications.viśiṣṭādvaita: Before each meal, recognise that the nourishment on your plate is the overflow of Bhagavān's apratihata-sāmarthya — his never-obstructed capacity — acting through earth and plant as his own body. Eating becomes a conscious participation in the Lord's self-sustaining care.
everyday_applications.dvaita: In moments of exhaustion or helplessness, Madhva's frame offers not self-effort but surrender to Hari's absolute sovereignty: every cell in your body is upheld by him, not by your will. The appropriate response is not striving but grateful, dependent worship (bhajana).
everyday_applications.śuddhādvaita: Vallabha's Soma is the flavour of life itself as Kṛṣṇa's delight. Practise finding in the taste of food, in the colour of a fruit, in the smell of rain-on-earth, not sensory data but Kṛṣṇa's rasa pressing outward into your experience.
everyday_applications.bhakti: Śrīdhara's 'aham eva saṃvardhayāmi' — 'I alone cause to flourish' — reframes all growth, every recovery from illness, every harvest after drought, as Bhagavān's personal act of nurturing. Gratitude, then, is not sentiment but accuracy.
everyday_applications.advaita-bhakti: Madhusūdana's earth-crumbling image is a contemplative exercise: for one minute, vividly imagine the ground beneath you without the divine ojas — it disintegrates. Then feel it cohere again. The stability you take for granted is moment-to-moment grace; walk accordingly.
